                        <li><strong>Richer context:</strong> A picture is worth a thousand tokens of description you don't have to write</li>
                    </ul>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center; justify-content: center;">
                    <div class="equation" style="width: 100%;">
                        <div class="formula" style="font-size: 1.3rem;">Image + Text → Understanding</div>
                        <p class="explain">Not image-to-text + text-to-understanding anymore</p>
                    </div>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- ============================================ -->
        <!-- INPUT LAYER TAKEAWAYS -->
        <!-- ============================================ -->

        <!-- SLIDE: Input Layer Takeaways -->
        <div class="slide slide-list">
            <h2 class="blue">Input Layer: Key Takeaways</h2>
            <ul>
                <li class="green"><strong>Let models write your prompts.</strong> Meta-prompting generates better prompts than manual crafting. Use it.</li>
                <li class="green"><strong>Automate prompt optimization.</strong> Tools like DSPy iterate faster than humans. Stop manual A/B testing.</li>
                <li class="green"><strong>Focus on context, not phrasing.</strong> What you put in the prompt matters more than how you say it.</li>
                <li class="green"><strong>Plan for multimodal now.</strong> If your AI system only handles text, you're building technical debt.</li>
            </ul>
        </div>



        <!-- ============================================ -->
        <!-- SECTION 2: MODEL LAYER -->
        <!-- ============================================ -->

        <!-- SLIDE: Section Break -->
        <div class="slide slide-section">
            <p class="label green">Section 2</p>
            <h2>Model & Data Layer</h2>
            <p class="sub">From "bigger is better" to "think before you speak"</p>
            <div class="framework-container">
                <svg class="framework-svg" viewBox="0 0 950 480" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-width: 1100px;">
                    <g class="framework-layer dimmed"><rect x="20" y="15" width="750" height="95" rx="8" fill="#3b82f6" opacity="0.15" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="2"/><text x="30" y="38" fill="#3b82f6" font-weight="700" font-size="14">INPUT LAYER</text></g>
                    <g class="framework-layer">
                        <rect x="20" y="120" width="750" height="95" rx="8" fill="#10b981" opacity="0.15" stroke="#10b981" stroke-width="2"/>
                        <text x="30" y="143" fill="#10b981" font-weight="700" font-size="14">DATA AND MODEL LAYER</text>
                        <rect x="30" y="155" width="140" height="50" rx="6" fill="#10b981"/><text x="100" y="185" text-anchor="middle" fill="white" font-size="10" font-weight="500">System 2 Reasoning</text>
                        <rect x="180" y="155" width="100" height="50" rx="6" fill="#10b981"/><text x="230" y="185" text-anchor="middle" fill="white" font-size="10" font-weight="500">RLVR</text>
                        <rect x="290" y="155" width="120" height="50" rx="6" fill="#10b981"/><text x="350" y="185" text-anchor="middle" fill="white" font-size="10" font-weight="500">Long Context</text>
                        <rect x="420" y="155" width="120" height="50" rx="6" fill="#10b981"/><text x="480" y="185" text-anchor="middle" fill="white" font-size="10" font-weight="500">Quantization</text>
                        <rect x="550" y="155" width="210" height="50" rx="6" fill="#10b981"/><text x="655" y="185" text-anchor="middle" fill="white" font-size="10" font-weight="500">Fine-Tuning & Distillation</text>
                    </g>
                    <rect class="highlight-ring active" x="15" y="115" width="760" height="105" rx="12"/>
                    <g class="framework-layer dimmed"><rect x="20" y="225" width="750" height="95" rx="8" fill="#f97316" opacity="0.15" stroke="#f97316" stroke-width="2"/><text x="30" y="248" fill="#f97316" font-weight="700" font-size="14">APPLICATION LAYER</text></g>
                    <g class="framework-layer dimmed"><rect x="20" y="330" width="750" height="95" rx="8" fill="#8b5cf6" opacity="0.15" stroke="#8b5cf6" stroke-width="2"/><text x="30" y="353" fill="#8b5cf6" font-weight="700" font-size="14">OUTPUT LAYER</text></g>
                    <g class="framework-layer dimmed"><rect x="790" y="15" width="145" height="410" rx="8" fill="#ef4444" opacity="0.15" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="2"/><text x="862" y="220" text-anchor="middle" fill="#ef4444" font-weight="700" font-size="13">CHALLENGES</text></g>
                </svg>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Section Overview -->
        <div class="slide slide-list">
            <h2>What Changed in the Model Layer</h2>
            <ul>
                <li class="green"><strong>Models learned to think.</strong> System 2 reasoning emerged: models that allocate compute dynamically based on problem difficulty.</li>
                <li class="green"><strong>RLVR changed training.</strong> Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards proved you can train reasoning without human labels.</li>
                <li class="green"><strong>Context windows hit 1M tokens.</strong> But effective use of long context requires more than just bigger windows.</li>
                <li class="green"><strong>Efficiency became a priority.</strong> Quantization and distillation made frontier capabilities accessible on consumer hardware.</li>
            </ul>
        </div>

        <!-- ============================================ -->
        <!-- SYSTEM 1 → SYSTEM 2 REASONING -->
        <!-- ============================================ -->

        <!-- SLIDE: System 2 Intro -->
        <div class="slide slide-statement">
            <span class="topic-tag green">System 2 Reasoning</span>
            <h2>The biggest shift in 2025: models that <span class="green">think before they speak</span>.</h2>
            <p class="small">Instead of generating tokens as fast as possible, these models allocate more compute to harder problems. The result: dramatically better reasoning on complex tasks.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: System 1 vs System 2 -->
        <div class="slide slide-vs">
            <span class="topic-tag green">System 2 Reasoning</span>
            <div class="vs-box old">
                <span class="tag">System 1</span>
                <h3>Fast, Intuitive</h3>
                <p>Immediate responses. Pattern matching. Great for simple queries, but prone to confident errors on hard problems.</p>
            </div>
            <span class="vs-arrow">→</span>
            <div class="vs-box new">
                <span class="tag">System 2</span>
                <h3>Slow, Deliberate</h3>
                <p>Models allocate thinking time proportional to difficulty. More reliable on complex reasoning, but 3-5x slower.</p>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Why System 2 Matters -->
        <div class="slide slide-cards">
            <span class="topic-tag green">System 2 Reasoning</span>
            <h2>Why System 2 Reasoning Matters</h2>
            <div class="cards-grid stacked">
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--green);">
                    <h3>Dynamic Compute Allocation</h3>
                    <p>Simple questions get quick answers. Complex problems trigger extended reasoning chains. The model decides how hard to think based on the task.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--blue);">
                    <h3>Visible Thinking Process</h3>
                    <p>You can see the model's reasoning in its "thinking" tokens. This makes debugging easier and helps identify where reasoning goes wrong.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--purple);">
                    <h3>Trade Speed for Accuracy</h3>
                    <p>For tasks where correctness matters more than latency—code generation, complex analysis, multi-step reasoning—the tradeoff is worth it.</p>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Test-Time Compute -->
        <div class="slide slide-content" style="justify-content: center;">
            <span class="topic-tag green">System 2 Reasoning</span>
            <div class="equation" style="max-width: 850px; margin: 0 auto;">
                <p class="formula">Test-Time Compute = <span class="green">Thinking Time</span> × <span class="blue">Tokens</span></p>
                <p class="explain">The new scaling law: you can improve outputs by letting models think longer</p>
            </div>
            <div style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1.5rem; color: var(--text-muted); font-size: 1.05rem; max-width: 750px; margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto;">
                <p>2024's scaling law was about training compute. 2025's insight: <strong>inference compute matters too</strong>.</p>
                <p style="margin-top: 0.5rem;">Models can solve harder problems by spending more compute at inference time, not just at training time.</p>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- ============================================ -->
        <!-- RLVR -->
        <!-- ============================================ -->

        <!-- SLIDE: RLHF Context -->
        <div class="slide slide-statement">
            <span class="topic-tag green">RLVR</span>
            <h2>2024 was the year of RLHF.</h2>
            <p class="small">Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback. Humans rank model outputs. The model learns what humans prefer. This gave us helpful, harmless assistants—but it doesn't scale, and "sounds good" isn't the same as "is correct."</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: RLVR Intro -->
        <div class="slide slide-statement">
            <span class="topic-tag green">RLVR</span>
            <h2>2025 introduced RLVR: rewards you can verify automatically.</h2>
            <p class="small">Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards. Give the model problems with checkable answers—math proofs, code that compiles, logic puzzles. Tell it only right or wrong. No human labelers needed. Scales with compute, not headcount.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: RLHF vs RLVR Visual -->
        <div class="slide slide-visual">
            <span class="topic-tag green">RLVR</span>
            <h2>RLHF vs RLVR: The Key Difference</h2>
            <img src="../images/rlhf_rlvr.png" alt="RLHF vs RLVR Comparison">
            <p class="caption">RLHF asks "which sounds better?" RLVR asks "is this correct?" One requires humans. One requires only a verifier.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: RLVR Insight -->
        <div class="slide slide-statement">
            <span class="topic-tag green">RLVR</span>
            <h2>RLVR compresses search into intuition.</h2>
            <p class="small">What looks like "reasoning" is actually learned search patterns. The model isn't thinking step-by-step—it's pattern matching on solution strategies it learned during training.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Self-Correction -->
        <div class="slide slide-content">
            <span class="topic-tag green">RLVR</span>
            <h2>The Self-Correction Breakthrough</h2>
            <div class="body">
                <div class="text">
                    <p>RLVR-trained models learned something unexpected: <strong>how to catch and correct their own mistakes</strong>.</p>
                    <ul>
                        <li>Models detect when reasoning is going wrong</li>
                        <li>They backtrack and try different approaches</li>
                        <li>This emerged naturally from the training process</li>
                    </ul>
                    <p style="margin-top: 1rem;"><strong>The results:</strong></p>
                    <ul>
                        <li>40-60% fewer hallucinations in trained domains</li>
                        <li>Models express uncertainty instead of fabricating</li>
                        <li>Graceful degradation on hard problems</li>
                    </ul>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="flex-direction: column; gap: 16px;">
                    <div class="card" style="background: rgba(16, 185, 129, 0.1); border-color: rgba(16, 185, 129, 0.3); text-align: center; width: 100%;">
                        <p style="font-size: 0.85rem; color: var(--green); margin-bottom: 0.5rem;">RLVR excels at</p>
                        <p style="font-size: 1.05rem;">Code • Math • Logic • Structured Tasks</p>
                    </div>
                    <div class="card" style="background: rgba(239, 68, 68, 0.1); border-color: rgba(239, 68, 68, 0.3); text-align: center; width: 100%;">
                        <p style="font-size: 0.85rem; color: var(--red); margin-bottom: 0.5rem;">RLVR struggles with</p>
                        <p style="font-size: 1.05rem;">Creative Writing • Subjective Tasks</p>
                    </div>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- ============================================ -->
        <!-- LONG CONTEXT -->
        <!-- ============================================ -->

        <!-- SLIDE: Long Context Intro -->
        <div class="slide slide-stat">
            <span class="topic-tag green">Long Context</span>
            <p class="num green">1M</p>
            <p class="label">tokens in a single context window</p>
            <p class="context">That's ~700 pages. Entire codebases. Full research papers with all citations. But there's a catch.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Context Windows -->
        <div class="slide slide-cards">
            <span class="topic-tag green">Long Context</span>
            <h2>Context Windows Exploded in 2025</h2>
            <div class="cards-grid">
                <div class="card" style="text-align: center;">
                    <p class="num green">1M</p>
                    <p style="font-weight: 600;">Gemini 3 Pro</p>
                    <p style="font-size: 0.85rem; color: var(--muted);">~700 pages input</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="text-align: center;">
                    <p class="num blue">400K</p>
                    <p style="font-weight: 600;">GPT-5.2</p>
                    <p style="font-size: 0.85rem; color: var(--muted);">~128K output cap</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="text-align: center;">
                    <p class="num purple">200K</p>
                    <p style="font-weight: 600;">Claude Opus 4.5</p>
                    <p style="font-size: 0.85rem; color: var(--muted);">Up to 1M enterprise</p>
                </div>
            </div>
            <p class="subtext muted" style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1.5rem;">Entire codebases in context. Multi-document analysis without chunking. Complex reasoning across long dependencies.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Effective vs Claimed -->
        <div class="slide slide-statement">
            <span class="topic-tag green">Long Context</span>
            <h2>Claimed context ≠ effective context.</h2>
            <p class="small">Models can accept 1M tokens. That doesn't mean they use them well. Information in the middle gets lost. Retrieval quality degrades with distance. Test your specific use case.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Long Context Challenges -->
        <div class="slide slide-list">
            <span class="topic-tag green">Long Context</span>
            <h2>The Long Context Reality Check</h2>
            <ul>
                <li class="orange"><strong>"Lost in the middle" problem persists.</strong> Models remember beginnings and ends better than middles. Structure your context accordingly.</li>
                <li class="blue"><strong>Costs scale linearly.</strong> 10x more context = 10x higher cost. Strategic context management still matters.</li>
                <li class="purple"><strong>Latency increases.</strong> Longer context means slower first-token response. Plan for user experience.</li>
                <li class="green"><strong>Quality varies by model.</strong> Some models handle 1M well. Others degrade at 100K. Benchmark your specific tasks.</li>
            </ul>
        </div>

        <!-- ============================================ -->
        <!-- EFFICIENCY & QUANTIZATION -->
        <!-- ============================================ -->

        <!-- SLIDE: Efficiency Intro -->
        <div class="slide slide-statement">
            <span class="topic-tag green">Efficiency</span>
            <h2>2025's hidden story: frontier capabilities on consumer hardware.</h2>
            <p class="small">Quantization, distillation, and mixture-of-experts made models 10x more accessible.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Quantization -->
        <div class="slide slide-visual">
            <span class="topic-tag green">Efficiency</span>
            <h2>Quantization: Smaller Without Losing Quality</h2>
            <img src="../images/quantization.png" alt="Quantization comparison showing 32-bit, 8-bit, and 4-bit models">
            <p class="caption">Reduce precision from 32-bit to 4-bit. Same model, 8x smaller, runs on consumer hardware. Quality loss is minimal for most production tasks.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- ============================================ -->
        <!-- FINE-TUNING & DISTILLATION -->
        <!-- ============================================ -->

        <!-- SLIDE: Fine-tuning Intro -->
        <div class="slide slide-statement">
            <span class="topic-tag green">Fine-Tuning</span>
            <h2>Fine-tuning: training a model on your specific data.</h2>
            <p class="small">Take a general-purpose model. Train it further on domain-specific examples. The result: a model that speaks your industry's language, follows your formats, and understands your context—often matching larger models at a fraction of the cost.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Fine-tuning in Practice -->
        <div class="slide slide-list">
            <span class="topic-tag green">Fine-Tuning</span>
            <h2>Where Fine-Tuning Made the Difference in 2025</h2>
            <ul>
                <li class="green"><strong>Healthcare:</strong> Medical records have unique structures, abbreviations, and terminology. Fine-tuned models outperformed general models on clinical tasks with less bias.</li>
                <li class="blue"><strong>Finance:</strong> Internal terminology in earnings reports and risk assessments that general models couldn't parse. Domain-specific fine-tuning unlocked understanding.</li>
                <li class="purple"><strong>Legal:</strong> Compliance and regulatory interpretation requires jurisdiction-specific knowledge that general models consistently miss.</li>
                <li class="orange"><strong>Scientific Research:</strong> Molecular science, drug discovery, and chemistry tasks where specialized notation and domain knowledge are essential.</li>
            </ul>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Fine-tuning Guidance -->
        <div class="slide slide-statement">
            <span class="topic-tag green">Fine-Tuning</span>
            <h2>But always start with prompting. Fine-tune only when you have to.</h2>
            <p class="small">Prompting is faster to iterate, requires no training data, and works for most use cases. Fine-tune when you're running the same task at massive scale, need consistent output formats, or require domain knowledge the base model lacks.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Distillation Trend -->
        <div class="slide slide-statement">
            <span class="topic-tag green">Distillation</span>
            <h2>Distillation became the default deployment strategy.</h2>
            <p class="small">Use a large model to generate training data. Train a smaller model on that data. Deploy the small model at 10x lower cost. This pattern—70B teacher to 7B student—drove most production cost optimizations in 2025.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Domain Experts -->
        <div class="slide slide-cards">
            <span class="topic-tag green">Distillation</span>
            <h2>Where Domain-Specific Models Shine</h2>
            <div class="cards-grid two">
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--green);">
                    <h3>Healthcare</h3>
                    <p>Medical coding from clinical notes. Drug interaction checking. Radiology report generation. Anywhere regulatory precision matters.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--blue);">
                    <h3>Legal</h3>
                    <p>Contract clause extraction. Case law research. Compliance document review. Tasks requiring jurisdiction-specific knowledge.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--purple);">
                    <h3>Finance</h3>
                    <p>Earnings call summarization. Risk factor analysis. Regulatory filing generation. Domain jargon and format requirements.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--orange);">
                    <h3>Code</h3>
                    <p>Repository-specific assistants. Internal API documentation. Company coding standards enforcement. Codebase-aware refactoring.</p>
                </div>
            </div>
            <p class="subtext muted" style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1rem;">The pattern: General models for exploration, specialized models for production.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- ============================================ -->
        <!-- TAKEAWAYS -->
        <!-- ============================================ -->

        <!-- SLIDE: Model Takeaways -->
        <div class="slide slide-list">
            <h2 class="green">Model Layer: Key Takeaways</h2>
            <ul>
                <li class="green"><strong>System 2 reasoning trades speed for accuracy.</strong> Use thinking models for complex tasks where correctness matters more than latency.</li>
                <li class="green"><strong>RLVR enables self-correction.</strong> Models trained with verifiable rewards catch their own mistakes on structured tasks.</li>
                <li class="green"><strong>Long context ≠ infinite context.</strong> Test effective context length for your use case. The middle gets lost.</li>
                <li class="green"><strong>Small + specialized beats large + general.</strong> Fine-tuned 7B often outperforms 70B at 10% the cost.</li>
            </ul>
        </div>



        <!-- ============================================ -->
        <!-- SECTION 3: APPLICATION LAYER -->
        <!-- ============================================ -->

        <!-- SLIDE: Section Break -->
        <div class="slide slide-section">
            <p class="label orange">Section 3</p>
            <h2>Application Layer</h2>
            <p class="sub">From "Which model?" to "Can it do real work?"</p>
            <div class="framework-container">
                <svg class="framework-svg" viewBox="0 0 950 480" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="max-width: 1100px;">
                    <g class="framework-layer dimmed"><rect x="20" y="15" width="750" height="95" rx="8" fill="#3b82f6" opacity="0.15" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="2"/><text x="30" y="38" fill="#3b82f6" font-weight="700" font-size="14">INPUT LAYER</text></g>
                    <g class="framework-layer dimmed"><rect x="20" y="120" width="750" height="95" rx="8" fill="#10b981" opacity="0.15" stroke="#10b981" stroke-width="2"/><text x="30" y="143" fill="#10b981" font-weight="700" font-size="14">DATA AND MODEL LAYER</text></g>
                    <g class="framework-layer">
                        <rect x="20" y="225" width="750" height="95" rx="8" fill="#f97316" opacity="0.15" stroke="#f97316" stroke-width="2"/>
                        <text x="30" y="248" fill="#f97316" font-weight="700" font-size="14">APPLICATION LAYER</text>
                        <rect x="30" y="260" width="160" height="50" rx="6" fill="#f97316"/><text x="110" y="290" text-anchor="middle" fill="white" font-size="11" font-weight="500">RAG</text>
                        <rect x="205" y="260" width="160" height="50" rx="6" fill="#f97316"/><text x="285" y="290" text-anchor="middle" fill="white" font-size="11" font-weight="500">Agents</text>
                        <rect x="380" y="260" width="160" height="50" rx="6" fill="#f97316"/><text x="460" y="290" text-anchor="middle" fill="white" font-size="9" font-weight="500">Tools / Skills / Standards</text>
                        <rect x="555" y="260" width="160" height="50" rx="6" fill="#f97316"/><text x="635" y="290" text-anchor="middle" fill="white" font-size="9" font-weight="500">Agentic Frameworks</text>
                    </g>
                    <rect class="highlight-ring active" x="15" y="220" width="760" height="105" rx="12"/>
                    <g class="framework-layer dimmed"><rect x="20" y="330" width="750" height="95" rx="8" fill="#8b5cf6" opacity="0.15" stroke="#8b5cf6" stroke-width="2"/><text x="30" y="353" fill="#8b5cf6" font-weight="700" font-size="14">OUTPUT LAYER</text></g>
                    <g class="framework-layer dimmed"><rect x="790" y="15" width="145" height="410" rx="8" fill="#ef4444" opacity="0.15" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="2"/><text x="862" y="220" text-anchor="middle" fill="#ef4444" font-weight="700" font-size="13">CHALLENGES</text></g>
                </svg>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Section Overview -->
        <div class="slide slide-cards">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Application</span>
            <h2>What Changed in the Application Layer</h2>
            <div class="cards-grid two">
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--blue);">
                    <h3>Delegation Replaced Answers</h3>
                    <p>Success shifted from “good responses” to “completed outcomes.”</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--green);">
                    <h3>RAG Became Infrastructure</h3>
                    <p>Hybrid retrieval, reranking, and structure-aware pipelines replaced naive chunking.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--purple);">
                    <h3>Agent Types Diverged</h3>
                    <p>Deep research, ambient automation, computer-use, and coding became distinct surfaces.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--orange);">
                    <h3>Standards Consolidated</h3>
                    <p>MCP + A2A shifted into open governance; fragmentation started to recede.</p>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- ============================================ -->
        <!-- RAG IN 2025 -->
        <!-- ============================================ -->

        <!-- SLIDE: Flashback -->
        <div class="slide slide-content">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">RAG</span>
            <h2>Flashback: What RAG Is</h2>
            <div class="body">
                <div class="text">
                    <p><strong>RAG = Retrieve → Augment → Generate</strong></p>
                    <ul>
                        <li><strong>Retrieve:</strong> pull the most relevant chunks from your knowledge base</li>
                        <li><strong>Augment:</strong> inject those chunks into the model’s context</li>
                        <li><strong>Generate:</strong> answer using retrieved evidence (ideally with citations)</li>
                    </ul>
                    <p style="margin-top: 1rem;"><strong>Naive RAG</strong> meant one-shot retrieval and hope. It breaks on synthesis, drift, and noisy chunks.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center;">
                    <img src="images/pdf_rag_layout_parser_pipeline.png" alt="Naive RAG pipeline" style="max-height: 52vh;">
                    <p style="font-size: 0.7rem; color: #888; margin-top: 0.5rem;">Source: <a href="https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/ai-machine-learning/build-retrieval-augmented-generation-rag-using-vertex-ai-search-and-gecko" target="_blank" style="color: #aaa;">Google Cloud</a></p>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Long Context Hype -->
        <div class="slide slide-statement">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">RAG</span>
            <h2>Then context windows increased—and people assumed RAG was over.</h2>
            <p class="small">If you can fit a whole corpus into context, why retrieve at all? That was the belief. Reality was messier: cost, freshness, permissions, and noise didn’t disappear.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: RAG Didn't Die -->
        <div class="slide slide-statement">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">RAG</span>
            <h2>RAG didn't die. Naive RAG did.</h2>
            <p class="small">Long context is a bigger desk. RAG is still choosing the right papers to put on it—and doing so under real-world constraints.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Why RAG Stayed -->
        <div class="slide slide-content">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">RAG</span>
            <h2>Why Retrieval Stayed Relevant</h2>
            <div class="body">
                <div class="text">
                    <ul>
                        <li class="blue"><strong>Cost control.</strong> Huge context windows are expensive. Retrieval lets you pay only for what you need.</li>
                        <li class="green"><strong>Freshness.</strong> If data changes daily, you don't want to keep repacking massive context. Fetch what's current.</li>
                        <li class="purple"><strong>Access control.</strong> "Put it all in the prompt" breaks down when different users have different permissions.</li>
                        <li class="orange"><strong>Auditability.</strong> Retrieval makes it easier to show what sources were used and why.</li>
                        <li class="orange"><strong>Long‑context reality:</strong> Databricks finds performance often peaks, then degrades as context grows—effective context is shorter than the max window.</li>
                    </ul>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center;">
                    <img src="images/databricks_long_context_performance.png" alt="Long context performance curve" style="max-height: 52vh;">
                    <p style="font-size: 0.7rem; color: #888; margin-top: 0.5rem;">Source: <a href="https://www.databricks.com/blog/long-context-rag-research-brief" target="_blank" style="color: #aaa;">Databricks Research Brief</a></p>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE 43e: RAG Theme 1 -->
        <div class="slide slide-content">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">RAG</span>
            <h2>RAG Grew Up: Structure Beats Chunks</h2>
            <div class="body">
                <div class="text">
                    <p class="muted" style="font-size: 0.85rem; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.12em;">What it is</p>
                    <div class="stats-row">
                        <div class="stat-box">
                            <div class="num">1</div>
                            <div class="label">Extract entities</div>
                        </div>
                        <div class="stat-box">
                            <div class="num">2</div>
                            <div class="label">Build graph</div>
                        </div>
                        <div class="stat-box">
                            <div class="num">3</div>
                            <div class="label">Summarize layers</div>
                        </div>
                        <div class="stat-box">
                            <div class="num">4</div>
                            <div class="label">Query top-down</div>
                        </div>
                    </div>
                    <div class="border-orange" style="margin-top: 1.25rem;">
                        <p><strong>Best for:</strong> policies, incident timelines, architecture tradeoffs</p>
                        <p><strong>Why it works:</strong> captures relationships before retrieval, not after</p>
                        <p><strong>When chunks win:</strong> narrow fact lookups with high precision</p>
                    </div>
                    <div class="takeaway" style="margin-top: 1.25rem; background: linear-gradient(135deg, rgba(249, 115, 22, 0.18), rgba(59, 130, 246, 0.12)); border: 1px solid rgba(249, 115, 22, 0.35);">
                        <h3 style="color: var(--orange);">2025 trend</h3>
                        <p>GraphRAG-style pipelines became shippable OSS and moved from research to production for synthesis-heavy questions.</p>
                    </div>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center;">
                    <img src="images/graphrag_pipeline.png" alt="GraphRAG pipeline" style="max-height: 52vh;">
                    <p style="font-size: 0.7rem; color: #888; margin-top: 0.5rem;">Source: <a href="https://livebook.manning.com/book/essential-graphrag/chapter-7" target="_blank" style="color: #aaa;">Essential GraphRAG (Manning)</a></p>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE 43f: RAG Theme 2 -->
        <div class="slide slide-content">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">RAG</span>
            <h2>Retrieval Became Agentic</h2>
            <div class="body">
                <div class="text">
                    <p class="muted" style="font-size: 0.85rem; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.12em;">What it is</p>
                    <div class="cards-grid" style="grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr); gap: 14px; margin-top: 0.5rem;">
                        <div class="card" style="border-top: 3px solid var(--blue);">
                            <h3>Plan</h3>
                            <p>Rewrite into focused sub-queries</p>
                        </div>
                        <div class="card" style="border-top: 3px solid var(--orange);">
                            <h3>Retrieve</h3>
                            <p>Parallel search across text + vectors</p>
                        </div>
                        <div class="card" style="border-top: 3px solid var(--green);">
                            <h3>Fuse</h3>
                            <p>Rerank and synthesize grounded context</p>
                        </div>
                    </div>
                    <div class="takeaway" style="margin-top: 1.25rem; background: linear-gradient(135deg, rgba(59, 130, 246, 0.16), rgba(16, 185, 129, 0.12)); border: 1px solid rgba(59, 130, 246, 0.35);">
                        <h3 style="color: var(--blue);">2025 trend</h3>
                        <p>Agentic retrieval shipped as platform features, with “retrieval reasoning effort” knobs and built-in semantic ranking.</p>
                    </div>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center;">
                    <img src="images/agentic_retrieval_azure.png" alt="Agentic retrieval architecture" style="max-height: 52vh;">
                    <p style="font-size: 0.7rem; color: #888; margin-top: 0.5rem;">Source: <a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/agentic-retrieval-overview" target="_blank" style="color: #aaa;">Microsoft Azure AI Search</a></p>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE 43g: RAG Theme 3 -->
        <div class="slide slide-content">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">RAG</span>
            <h2>Multimodal/PDF RAG: Parsing Became the Work</h2>
            <div class="body">
                <div class="text">
                    <p class="muted" style="font-size: 0.85rem; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.12em;">What it is</p>
                    <div class="stats-row">
                        <div class="stat-box">
                            <div class="num">1</div>
                            <div class="label">Parse layout</div>
                        </div>
                        <div class="stat-box">
                            <div class="num">2</div>
                            <div class="label">Handle images</div>
                        </div>
                        <div class="stat-box">
                            <div class="num">3</div>
                            <div class="label">Index by structure</div>
                        </div>
                    </div>
                    <div class="cards-grid two" style="margin-top: 1rem;">
                        <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--blue);">
                            <h3>Image → Text</h3>
                            <p>Caption/OCR and index as text for retrieval</p>
                        </div>
                        <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--purple);">
                            <h3>Image → Vector</h3>
                            <p>Embed with multimodal models for direct search</p>
                        </div>
                    </div>
                    <div class="takeaway" style="margin-top: 1.25rem; background: linear-gradient(135deg, rgba(139, 92, 246, 0.16), rgba(59, 130, 246, 0.12)); border: 1px solid rgba(139, 92, 246, 0.35);">
                        <h3 style="color: var(--purple);">2025 trend</h3>
                        <p>Hosted file search + parsers became standard. Extraction quality became the dominant bottleneck.</p>
                    </div>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center;">
                    <div class="card" style="max-width: 520px; text-align: left; border-left: 4px solid var(--purple);">
                        <h3>Parsing Stack</h3>
                        <p><strong>Layout:</strong> sections, tables, headings, footnotes</p>
                        <p><strong>Images:</strong> OCR + captioning + diagram text</p>
                        <p><strong>Chunking:</strong> structure-aware splits for clean retrieval</p>
                    </div>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE 43h: RAG Theme 4 -->
        <div class="slide slide-content">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">RAG</span>
            <h2>RAG Moved Into Platforms</h2>
            <div class="body">
                <div class="text">
                    <p class="muted" style="font-size: 0.85rem; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.12em;">What it is</p>
                    <div class="cards-grid" style="grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr); gap: 14px; margin-top: 0.5rem;">
                        <div class="card" style="border-top: 3px solid var(--blue);">
                            <h3>Managed RAG Engines</h3>
                            <p>Managed vector DB + retrieval strategies (KNN/ANN) with tunable index parameters.</p>
                        </div>
                        <div class="card" style="border-top: 3px solid var(--green);">
                            <h3>Hosted File Search</h3>
                            <p>Vector stores that auto‑parse/chunk/embed, with query rewrite + keyword/semantic search and reranking.</p>
                        </div>
                        <div class="card" style="border-top: 3px solid var(--purple);">
                            <h3>Warehouse‑Native RAG</h3>
                            <p>Hybrid retrieval + semantic reranking built into governed data platforms.</p>
                        </div>
                    </div>
                    <div class="border-orange" style="margin-top: 1.25rem;">
                        <p><strong>Platform primitives now include:</strong> vector storage, retrieval strategy, and retrieval controls</p>
                        <p><strong>Governance pressure:</strong> keep retrieval near data, reuse platform security and access controls</p>
                    </div>
                    <div class="takeaway" style="margin-top: 1.25rem; background: linear-gradient(135deg, rgba(16, 185, 129, 0.16), rgba(59, 130, 246, 0.12)); border: 1px solid rgba(16, 185, 129, 0.35);">
                        <h3 style="color: var(--green);">2025 trend</h3>
                        <p>Buy vs build shifted: teams start with managed RAG engines, hosted file search, or warehouse‑native search—and customize only where needed.</p>
                    </div>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center;">
                    <div class="card" style="max-width: 520px; text-align: left; border-left: 4px solid var(--green);">
                        <h3>Examples</h3>
                        <p><strong>Vertex AI RAG Engine:</strong> managed vector storage, chunking, and retrieval strategies</p>
                        <p><strong>OpenAI File Search:</strong> auto parsing/chunking + keyword/semantic search + reranking</p>
                        <p><strong>Snowflake Cortex Search:</strong> hybrid retrieval with semantic reranking built in</p>
                    </div>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE 43i: RAG Theme 5 -->
        <div class="slide slide-content">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">RAG</span>
            <h2>Hybrid + Reranking Is the Baseline</h2>
            <div class="body">
                <div class="text">
                    <p class="muted" style="font-size: 0.85rem; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.12em;">What it is</p>
                    <div class="stats-row">
                        <div class="stat-box">
                            <div class="num">1</div>
                            <div class="label">BM25 + Vector</div>
                        </div>
                        <div class="stat-box">
                            <div class="num">2</div>
                            <div class="label">RRF Fusion</div>
                        </div>
                        <div class="stat-box">
                            <div class="num">3</div>
                            <div class="label">Semantic Rerank</div>
                        </div>
                    </div>
                    <div class="cards-grid two" style="margin-top: 1rem;">
                        <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--blue);">
                            <h3>Why hybrid</h3>
                            <p>Keyword hits + semantic similarity raises recall on real queries</p>
                        </div>
                        <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--orange);">
                            <h3>Why rerank</h3>
                            <p>Second‑stage ranking improves precision on the short list</p>
                        </div>
                    </div>
                    <div class="border-orange" style="margin-top: 1.25rem;">
                        <p><strong>Knobs that matter:</strong> text recall window (maxTextRecallSize), RRF fusion, and reranker on/off</p>
                        <p><strong>Where it shows up:</strong> hybrid queries fuse with RRF, then semantic rankers rerank top results</p>
                    </div>
                    <div class="takeaway" style="margin-top: 1.25rem; background: linear-gradient(135deg, rgba(59, 130, 246, 0.16), rgba(249, 115, 22, 0.12)); border: 1px solid rgba(59, 130, 246, 0.35);">
                        <h3 style="color: var(--blue);">2025 trend</h3>
                        <p>Hybrid + rerank shipped as defaults across platforms; retrieval quality became tunable engineering, not guesswork.</p>
                    </div>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center;">
                    <div class="card" style="max-width: 520px; text-align: left; border-left: 4px solid var(--blue);">
                        <h3>Where it’s baked in</h3>
                        <p><strong>Azure AI Search:</strong> RRF fusion for hybrid results + semantic reranker on top</p>
                        <p><strong>Amazon Bedrock KB:</strong> reranker models can be applied during retrieval</p>
                        <p><strong>Snowflake Cortex Search:</strong> hybrid retrieval + semantic reranking by default</p>
                    </div>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- ============================================ -->
        <!-- AGENT TYPES -->
        <!-- ============================================ -->

        <!-- SLIDE: Agents Became a Product Category -->
        <div class="slide slide-cards">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Agents</span>
            <h2>2025 Was the Year of Agents</h2>
            <div class="cards-grid four">
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--blue);">
                    <h3>Research Agents</h3>
                    <p>Multi‑step analysis that produces auditable reports and citations.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--green);">
                    <h3>Computer‑Use Agents</h3>
                    <p>Browser + UI automation when APIs don’t exist.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--purple);">
                    <h3>Coding Agents</h3>
                    <p>IDE, terminal, and PR surfaces for real engineering work.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--orange);">
                    <h3>Workflow Agents</h3>
                    <p>Ops/support/app‑building workflows with reviewable outputs.</p>
                </div>
            </div>
            <p class="subtext">The signal: agents shipped across categories, not just in one standout demo.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: T-Shape Year -->
        <div class="slide slide-two-col">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Agents</span>
            <h2>2025’s T‑Shape: Wide Wins + A Few Deep Wins</h2>
            <div class="cols">
                <div class="col">
                    <h3 class="green">Wide (Shallow) Wins</h3>
                    <ul>
                        <li>Bounded workflows with review gates</li>
                        <li>Customer support, IT/service desk, internal ops</li>
                        <li>Value came from speed + coverage, not autonomy</li>
                    </ul>
                </div>
                <div class="col">
                    <h3 class="blue">Deep (Vertical) Wins</h3>
                    <ul>
                        <li>Auditable deliverables (citations, PRs, logs)</li>
                        <li>Deep Research‑style work where “good enough” still helps</li>
                        <li>Fewer domains, much higher ROI when it hits</li>
                    </ul>
                </div>
            </div>
            <p class="subtext">Reality check: many projects stalled when ROI and reliability weren’t clear.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Agent Types Intro -->
        <div class="slide slide-statement">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Agents</span>
            <h2>2025 was the year agent work split into distinct categories.</h2>
            <p class="small">"Agent" stopped meaning "LLM that can call a tool" and started meaning "a system that can complete work across many steps, over time, with integration, and with guardrails."</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Deep Agents -->
        <div class="slide slide-content">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Agents</span>
            <h2>Deep Agents: Long-Horizon Work</h2>
            <div class="body">
                <div class="text">
                    <p>Deep agents handle tasks that take minutes to hours, with many steps, context management, and delegation.</p>
                    <p style="margin-top: 1rem;"><strong>Methodology: Plan → Delegate → Verify</strong></p>
                    <ul>
                        <li><strong>Plan:</strong> break goals into verifiable subtasks</li>
                        <li><strong>Delegate:</strong> route work to subagents or tools</li>
                        <li><strong>Verify:</strong> check outputs before shipping</li>
                        <li><strong>State:</strong> persist artifacts, not just chat history</li>
                    </ul>
                    <p style="margin-top: 1rem;"><strong>Deep research products:</strong></p>
                    <ul>
                        <li>OpenAI Deep Research</li>
                        <li>Anthropic Research system (sub‑agents)</li>
                        <li>ChatGPT agent mode (research + action in one flow)</li>
                    </ul>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center;">
                    <img src="images/anthropic_research_subagents.png" alt="Anthropic research system with sub-agents" style="max-height: 52vh;">
                    <p style="font-size: 0.7rem; color: #888; margin-top: 0.5rem;">Source: <a href="https://www.anthropic.com/research/multi-agent-research-system" target="_blank" style="color: #aaa;">Anthropic Research</a></p>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Ambient & Background Agents -->
        <div class="slide slide-content">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Agents</span>
            <h2>Ambient & Background Agents: Always-On Automation</h2>
            <div class="body">
                <div class="text">
                    <p>Deep agents proved long‑horizon work. But most production volume shifted to ambient/background agents that act on events.</p>
                    <p style="margin-top: 1rem;"><strong>They respond to:</strong></p>
                    <ul>
                        <li>Event streams, logs, monitoring alerts</li>
                        <li>Tickets breaching SLA, churn signals spiking</li>
                        <li>Build failures, incident starts, contract renewals</li>
                    </ul>
                    <p style="margin-top: 1rem;"><strong>Core ingredients:</strong></p>
                    <ul>
                        <li>Triggers: event streams, schedules, webhooks</li>
                        <li>Policies: what it can do automatically vs. what needs approval</li>
                        <li>Memory of ongoing state: what's already handled</li>
                    </ul>
                    <p style="margin-top: 1rem;"><strong>Background mode:</strong> async delegation that returns reviewable artifacts (PRs, reports, tickets).</p>
                    <p style="margin-top: 1rem;"><strong>Why they work:</strong> bounded actions + review gates keep autonomy safe.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="flex-direction: column; gap: 16px; justify-content: center;">
                    <div class="card" style="background: rgba(139, 92, 246, 0.1); border-color: rgba(139, 92, 246, 0.3); text-align: center;">
                        <p style="font-size: 0.9rem; color: var(--purple); margin-bottom: 0.5rem;">Where they win</p>
                        <p style="font-size: 1rem;">IT ops triage • Security alert routing • SLA management • Compliance checks</p>
                    </div>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Computer Use -->
        <div class="slide slide-content">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Agents</span>
            <h2>Computer Use: UI Control When No API Exists</h2>
            <div class="body">
                <div class="text">
                    <p>Agents that operate the real surface area people use: browsers and SaaS UIs.</p>
                    <p style="margin-top: 1rem;"><strong>OpenAI Operator → ChatGPT agent mode</strong></p>
                    <ul>
                        <li>Uses screenshots to “see” and virtual mouse/keyboard to act</li>
                        <li>Books reservations, fills forms, places orders</li>
                        <li>Bridges research and action in one workflow</li>
                    </ul>
                    <p style="margin-top: 1rem;"><strong>Anthropic Computer Use</strong></p>
                    <ul>
                        <li>Developer‑facing tool for UI automation</li>
                        <li>Useful when no reliable API exists</li>
                    </ul>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="flex-direction: column; gap: 16px; justify-content: center;">
                    <div class="card" style="background: rgba(239, 68, 68, 0.1); border-color: rgba(239, 68, 68, 0.3); text-align: center;">
                        <p style="font-size: 0.9rem; color: var(--red); margin-bottom: 0.5rem;">The risk</p>
                        <p style="font-size: 1rem;">UI brittleness, broad access requirements, irreversible actions. Require approvals for anything permanent.</p>
                    </div>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Spec Clarification -->
        <div class="slide slide-statement">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Agents</span>
            <h2>The best agents ask questions before they act.</h2>
            <p class="small">A quiet 2025 shift: spec‑clarification became a built‑in step. Lovable shipped a “questions tool” because most agent mistakes start with missing requirements.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Multi-Agent -->
        <div class="slide slide-two-col">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Agents</span>
            <h2>Multi-Agent: When It Helps, When It Hurts</h2>
            <div class="cols">
                <div class="col">
                    <h3 class="green">When It Helps</h3>
                    <ul>
                        <li><strong>Parallel research:</strong> Multiple agents gather evidence simultaneously, then consolidate</li>
                        <li><strong>Role separation:</strong> Planner, executor, reviewer as distinct agents</li>
                        <li><strong>Tool specialization:</strong> Different agents with different permissions or domains</li>
                        <li><strong>Parallel attempts:</strong> Multiple solutions, choose the best</li>
                    </ul>
                </div>
                <div class="col">
                    <h3 class="red">When It Hurts</h3>
                    <ul>
                        <li><strong>Non-determinism:</strong> Outcomes vary more with multiple agents</li>
                        <li><strong>Coordination overhead:</strong> Agents disagree or duplicate work</li>
                        <li><strong>Error amplification:</strong> One agent's wrong assumption spreads</li>
                        <li><strong>Cost:</strong> You pay for parallel runs</li>
                    </ul>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>


        <!-- SLIDE: Coding Agents -->
        <div class="slide slide-statement">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Agents</span>
            <h2>Coding agents became the first place many teams experienced real agents.</h2>
            <p class="small">Why? Software work has the perfect control surface: repos, tests, CI, and pull requests. Every step is visible. Humans steer via PR review. Claude Code, Cursor, and Copilot made this real.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Coding Agent Types -->
        <div class="slide slide-cards">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Agents</span>
            <h2>Three Surfaces for Coding Agents</h2>
            <div class="cards-grid stacked">
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--blue);">
                    <h3>IDE Agents</h3>
                    <p>Multi-step work inside your editor. Finds files, edits across modules, runs tests, fixes and retries. Cursor’s agent-first IDE pushed this surface forward.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--green);">
                    <h3>Repo & PR Agents</h3>
                    <p>Work through issues and pull requests in CI. Produce PRs, logs, and reviewable commits. GitHub Copilot coding agent made this a mainstream workflow.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--purple);">
                    <h3>Terminal Agents</h3>
                    <p>Agentic coding from the command line. Delegates substantial engineering tasks from the terminal—Claude Code made this feel native.</p>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Coding Agents Timeline -->
        <div class="slide slide-content">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Agents</span>
            <h2>Coding Agents: From Snippets to Full Tasks</h2>
            <div class="body" style="flex-direction: column; gap: 18px;">
                <div class="text" style="width: 100%; display: flex; justify-content: space-between; gap: 24px; align-items: flex-start;">
                    <ul style="flex: 1; margin: 0;">
                        <li><strong>Then:</strong> tab completion and small snippet help</li>
                        <li><strong>Now:</strong> multi-file edits, tests, and CI-aware workflows</li>
                        <li><strong>Shift:</strong> from “assist in-editor” to “deliver reviewable work”</li>
                        <li><strong>Surface:</strong> PRs + agent workspaces became the control plane</li>
                    </ul>
                    <p class="subtext" style="max-width: 280px; margin: 0;">2025 was the year coding agents moved from suggestions to execution.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="width: 100%; display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center;">
                    <div style="display: flex; gap: 18px; align-items: center; justify-content: center; width: 100%;">
                        <img src="images/coding_agents_timeline_2025_v3_part1.png" alt="Coding agents evolution timeline (part 1)" style="height: 72vh; width: 49%; object-fit: contain;">
                        <img src="images/coding_agents_timeline_2025_v3_part2.png" alt="Coding agents evolution timeline (part 2)" style="height: 72vh; width: 49%; object-fit: contain;">
                    </div>
                    <p style="font-size: 0.7rem; color: #888; margin-top: 0.5rem;">Source: internal 2025 timeline</p>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Coding Agents Timeline 1 -->
        <div class="slide slide-content">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Agents</span>
            <h2>The Foundation: Tools Become Standard (Late 2024 → Early 2025)</h2>
            <div class="body">
                <div class="text">
                    <p><strong>Key beats</strong></p>
                    <ul>
                        <li>MCP made tool connectivity a standard (\"USB‑C for tools\")</li>
                        <li>Agent = model + tool protocol + runtime permissions</li>
                        <li>Integrations shifted from bespoke plugins to reusable wiring</li>
                        <li><strong>Product signal:</strong> MCP servers + registries became a real ecosystem</li>
                    </ul>
                    <p class="subtext">Once tools became standard, the rest of the stack could scale.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center; width: 100%; max-width: 520px;">
                    <div style="width: 100%;">
                        <div style="height: 6px; background: linear-gradient(90deg, var(--blue), var(--green)); border-radius: 999px;"></div>
                        <div style="display: flex; justify-content: space-between; margin-top: 8px; font-size: 0.8rem; color: var(--text-muted);">
                            <span>Nov 2024</span>
                            <span>Feb 2025</span>
                        </div>
                    </div>
                    <div class="cards-grid two" style="margin-top: 14px;">
                        <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--blue);">
                            <h3>Tool Hub</h3>
                            <p>GitHub • Jira • DB • Slack</p>
                        </div>
                        <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--green);">
                            <h3>Client Surfaces</h3>
                            <p>IDE • Terminal • Web</p>
                        </div>
                    </div>
                    <p style="font-size: 0.7rem; color: #888; margin-top: 0.5rem;">Standard tool wiring</p>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Coding Agents Timeline 2 -->
        <div class="slide slide-content">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Agents</span>
            <h2>From Chat to Execution Loops (Feb → Jun 2025)</h2>
            <div class="body">
                <div class="text">
                    <p><strong>Key beats</strong></p>
                    <ul>
                        <li><strong>Claude Code:</strong> terminal‑native agent workflow (edit, run tests, commit)</li>
                        <li><strong>Codex CLI + cloud tasks:</strong> delegated work with logs + patches</li>
                        <li>Dev loop shifts to: plan → edit → run → verify → commit</li>
                    </ul>
                    <p class="subtext">You stop copying snippets; you start handing off tasks.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="display: flex; gap: 16px; align-items: center; justify-content: center; width: 100%;">
                    <div class="card" style="flex: 1; border-left: 4px solid var(--blue); font-family: 'SF Mono', Monaco, monospace; font-size: 0.85rem;">
                        <p>$ run tests</p>
                        <p>✓ 48 passed</p>
                        <p>$ edit module</p>
                        <p>✓ patch ready</p>
                        <p>$ git status</p>
                    </div>
                    <div class="card" style="flex: 1; border-left: 4px solid var(--orange); text-align: center;">
                        <p style="font-weight: 700; color: var(--orange);">Plan</p>
                        <p style="font-size: 0.9rem;">↓</p>
                        <p style="font-weight: 700; color: var(--orange);">Implement</p>
                        <p style="font-size: 0.9rem;">↓</p>
                        <p style="font-weight: 700; color: var(--orange);">Test</p>
                        <p style="font-size: 0.9rem;">↓</p>
                        <p style="font-weight: 700; color: var(--orange);">Review</p>
                    </div>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Coding Agents Timeline 3 -->
        <div class="slide slide-content">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Agents</span>
            <h2>Control + Quality at Speed (Jul → Sep 2025)</h2>
            <div class="body">
                <div class="text">
                    <p><strong>Key beats</strong></p>
                    <ul>
                        <li><strong>Cursor:</strong> To‑dos + queues made long tasks steerable</li>
                        <li><strong>Bugbot / agent review:</strong> scaled PR quality checks</li>
                        <li><strong>Session resume:</strong> longer context + memory reduced “agent forgot”</li>
                    </ul>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="align-items: flex-start;">
                    <div class="cards-grid" style="width: 100%; gap: 18px; align-items: start;">
                        <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--blue); background: linear-gradient(180deg, rgba(59,130,246,0.12), rgba(10,10,10,0.2)); padding: 20px;">
                            <h3 style="color: var(--blue); margin-bottom: 10px;">Steerability</h3>
                            <div style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 8px; font-size: 0.9rem;">
                                <div style="display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px;"><span style="width: 12px; height: 12px; border: 1px solid var(--blue); border-radius: 3px;"></span><span>Queue next task</span></div>
                                <div style="display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px;"><span style="width: 12px; height: 12px; border: 1px solid var(--blue); border-radius: 3px;"></span><span>Review intermediate plan</span></div>
                                <div style="display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px;"><span style="width: 12px; height: 12px; border: 1px solid var(--blue); border-radius: 3px;"></span><span>Resume with memory</span></div>
                            </div>
                            <div style="margin-top: 12px; padding: 10px; border-radius: 10px; background: #0b1220; border: 1px solid rgba(59,130,246,0.35); font-family: 'SF Mono', Monaco, monospace; font-size: 0.75rem;">
                                <div style="color: #60a5fa;">TODO: refactor auth flow</div>
                                <div style="color: #94a3b8;">TODO: add retry tests</div>
                            </div>
                        </div>
                        <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--green); background: linear-gradient(180deg, rgba(16,185,129,0.12), rgba(10,10,10,0.2)); padding: 20px;">
                            <h3 style="color: var(--green); margin-bottom: 10px;">PR Guardrails</h3>
                            <div style="font-family: 'SF Mono', Monaco, monospace; font-size: 0.75rem; background: #0b1220; border: 1px solid rgba(16,185,129,0.35); border-radius: 10px; padding: 10px;">
                                <div style="color: #9ca3af;">+ if (!isValid) return err</div>
                                <div style="color: #9ca3af;">+ await saveRecord()</div>
                                <div style="margin-top: 8px; color: #34d399;">// agent review: missing retry path</div>
                                <div style="color: #34d399;">// add unit coverage for edge case</div>
                            </div>
                            <div style="margin-top: 10px; display: flex; gap: 8px; flex-wrap: wrap;">
                                <span style="padding: 4px 8px; border-radius: 999px; background: rgba(16,185,129,0.2); color: #34d399; font-size: 0.7rem;">logic</span>
                                <span style="padding: 4px 8px; border-radius: 999px; background: rgba(16,185,129,0.2); color: #34d399; font-size: 0.7rem;">security</span>
                                <span style="padding: 4px 8px; border-radius: 999px; background: rgba(16,185,129,0.2); color: #34d399; font-size: 0.7rem;">tests</span>
                            </div>
                        </div>
                        <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--purple); background: linear-gradient(180deg, rgba(139,92,246,0.12), rgba(10,10,10,0.2)); padding: 20px;">
                            <h3 style="color: var(--purple); margin-bottom: 10px;">Session Resume</h3>
                            <div style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 8px;">
                                <div style="display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px;">
                                    <span style="width: 8px; height: 8px; border-radius: 50%; background: var(--purple);"></span>
                                    <span style="font-size: 0.85rem;">Checkpoint: auth refactor</span>
                                </div>
                                <div style="display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px;">
                                    <span style="width: 8px; height: 8px; border-radius: 50%; background: var(--purple); opacity: 0.6;"></span>
                                    <span style="font-size: 0.85rem;">Context pack compressed</span>
                                </div>
                                <div style="display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px;">
                                    <span style="width: 8px; height: 8px; border-radius: 50%; background: var(--purple); opacity: 0.4;"></span>
                                    <span style="font-size: 0.85rem;">Resume work in new session</span>
                                </div>
                            </div>
                            <div style="margin-top: 12px; padding: 10px; border-radius: 10px; border: 1px solid rgba(139,92,246,0.35); background: #0b1220;">
                                <p style="font-size: 0.75rem; color: #c4b5fd; margin: 0;">Context snapshot · 12 files · 3 decisions</p>
                            </div>
                        </div>
                    </div>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Coding Agents Timeline 4 -->
        <div class="slide slide-content">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Agents</span>
            <h2>Scale & Reuse (Oct → Dec 2025)</h2>
            <div class="body">
                <div class="text">
                    <p><strong>Key beats</strong></p>
                    <ul>
                        <li>Workflow‑native agents fit real team processes</li>
                        <li>Parallel agents work in isolated worktrees</li>
                        <li>Skills/plugins package reusable competence</li>
                    </ul>
                    <p class="subtext">Organizations can standardize behavior instead of re‑prompting.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 12px; align-items: center;">
                    <div class="card" style="width: 100%; max-width: 520px; border-left: 4px solid var(--blue);">
                        <p><strong>Issue → @agent → sandbox run → PR → CI → review → merge</strong></p>
                    </div>
                    <div class="cards-grid three" style="max-width: 520px;">
                        <div class="card"><h3>Release PR</h3><p>Skills pack</p></div>
                        <div class="card"><h3>Migration</h3><p>Skills pack</p></div>
                        <div class="card"><h3>Test Fixer</h3><p>Skills pack</p></div>
                    </div>
                    <div class="card" style="max-width: 520px; text-align: center; border-left: 4px solid var(--purple);">
                        <p>Parallel agents fan‑out → isolated worktrees</p>
                    </div>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: Coding Agent Methodologies -->
        <div class="slide slide-content">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Agents</span>
            <h2>Methodologies for Building with Coding Agents</h2>
            <div class="body">
                <div class="text">
                    <div class="cards-grid stacked">
                        <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--blue);">
                            <h3>Spec‑Driven Development</h3>
                            <p>Write the spec first, then plan tasks, then implement. Keeps agent work aligned to intent.</p>
                            <p class="subtext">Common flow: specify → plan → tasks → implement.</p>
                        </div>
                        <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--green);">
                            <h3>Research → Plan → Implement</h3>
                            <p>Separate exploration from execution, then lock a plan before code changes.</p>
                            <p class="subtext">Human leverage points: review research + plan before commit.</p>
                        </div>
                        <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--purple);">
                            <h3>Verification‑First Loops</h3>
                            <p>Run/verify cycles keep agents honest—reproduce, patch, re‑run tests, report.</p>
                            <p class="subtext">Plan → edit → run → verify.</p>
                        </div>
                    </div>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 12px; align-items: center;">
                    <img src="images/humanlayer_human_leverage_review_points.png" alt="Human leverage review points for coding agents" style="width: 100%; max-width: 520px; border-radius: 12px; border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.08);">
                    <img src="images/humanlayer_human_leverage_compaction.png" alt="Human leverage for compressing context and checkpoints" style="width: 100%; max-width: 520px; border-radius: 12px; border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.08);">
                    <p style="font-size: 0.7rem; color: #888; margin-top: 0.2rem;">Source: <a href="https://github.com/humanlayer/advanced-context-engineering-for-coding-agents/blob/main/ace-fca.md#on-human-leverage" target="_blank" style="color: #aaa;">Humanlayer ACE</a></p>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>


        <!-- SLIDE: Enterprise Failure Modes -->
        <div class="slide slide-list">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Agents</span>
            <h2>Why Enterprise Agents Still Fail</h2>
            <ul>
                <li class="red"><strong>ROI + cost.</strong> Compute, integration, and oversight erase the business case.</li>
                <li class="orange"><strong>Reliability.</strong> Long-horizon drift, tool misuse, and hard-to-debug failures.</li>
                <li class="blue"><strong>Autonomy boundaries.</strong> Most teams require approval gates for write actions.</li>
                <li class="purple"><strong>Security.</strong> Prompt injection, data leakage, and tool abuse expand the attack surface.</li>
                <li class="green"><strong>Brittleness.</strong> UI changes and unstable workflows break agents in production.</li>
            </ul>
        </div>
        <!-- ============================================ -->
        <!-- SKILLS -->
        <!-- ============================================ -->

        <!-- SLIDE: Skills Compact -->
        <div class="slide slide-content">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Skills</span>
            <h2>Skills: Packaged Expertise for Agents</h2>
            <div class="body">
                <div class="text">
                    <div style="margin-bottom: 1.5rem;">
                        <h3 class="orange">Agents execute.</h3>
                        <p>They reason, call tools, handle multi-step workflows. But agents are general-purpose—they don't inherently know your domain.</p>
                    </div>
                    <div>
                        <h3 class="blue">Skills equip.</h3>
                        <p>Folders of instructions, scripts, and resources that agents load on demand. BigQuery queries. NDA review procedures. PDF extraction. Domain expertise, packaged and portable.</p>
                    </div>
                </div>
                <div class="visual" style="flex-direction: column;">
                    <img src="images/skills_architecture.webp" alt="Agent + Skills + Virtual Machine" style="max-height: 420px; width: 100%; object-fit: contain;">
                    <p class="caption">Agent configuration includes equipped skills and MCP servers. Skills live as directories in the agent's file system—loaded when relevant. Source: Anthropic</p>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- ============================================ -->
        <!-- AGENTIC FRAMEWORKS -->
        <!-- ============================================ -->

        <!-- SLIDE: Agentic Frameworks -->
        <div class="slide slide-statement">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Frameworks</span>
            <h2>Frameworks stopped being libraries. They became runtimes.</h2>
            <p class="small">Agents in 2025 aren’t just prompts and tools—they run inside systems built for state, control, and recovery.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: What Changed in Frameworks -->
        <div class="slide slide-cards">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Frameworks</span>
            <h2>What Changed in Agentic Frameworks</h2>
            <div class="cards-grid two">
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--blue);">
                    <h3>Explicit Workflows</h3>
                    <p>Event-driven graphs with named steps and typed state replaced ad-hoc loops. The flow is visible and debuggable.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--green);">
                    <h3>Durable State + Human Gates</h3>
                    <p>Checkpoint, pause, and resume became native. Humans can approve, redirect, or recover long-running tasks.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--purple);">
                    <h3>Scalable Tool Use</h3>
                    <p>Tool search + programmatic calling made orchestration explicit, cheaper, and more reliable than prompt-only routing.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--orange);">
                    <h3>Multi-Agent: Supported, Not Default</h3>
                    <p>Coordination primitives matured, but teams learned to use multi-agent as an advanced pattern, not the default.</p>
                </div>
                <div class="card" style="border-left: 4px solid var(--red);">
                    <h3>Tracing + Evals by Default</h3>
                    <p>Observability and quality scoring moved into the runtime so agents can be monitored and graded.</p>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>

        <!-- ============================================ -->
        <!-- STANDARDS -->
        <!-- ============================================ -->

        <!-- SLIDE: Why Standards Matter -->
        <div class="slide slide-statement">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Standards</span>
            <h2>2025 was the year everyone realized fragmentation would kill adoption.</h2>
            <p class="small">Two different interoperability problems emerged. How do AI apps talk to tools and data sources? How do agents coordinate with other agents? The industry started racing toward standard rails.</p>
        </div>

        <!-- SLIDE: MCP and A2A -->
        <div class="slide slide-two-col">
            <span class="topic-tag orange">Standards</span>
            <h2>Two Standards, Two Problems</h2>
            <div class="cols">
